subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Auroras: Light from Space Weather
description: A quick win: understand Auroras: Light from Space Weather.

hook.text: Auroras are glowing curtains of light near the poles, created when solar particles slam into Earth's atmosphere.

buildup.text: Charged particles from the solar wind follow Earth's magnetic field lines to the poles, where they hit gas molecules.

discovery.text: The collisions excite atmospheric gases. Oxygen glows green and red; nitrogen glows blue and purple.

twist.text: During strong solar storms, auroras can be seen at much lower latitudes. In 1859, auroras were visible in the Caribbean.

climax.text: Other planets have auroras too. Jupiter's are powered by Io's volcanic material, not just the solar wind.

punchline.text: Solar particles painting the sky. Earth's magnetic field turns danger into beauty.

quiz.question: What causes the different colors in an aurora?
options: Different atmospheric gases emit different colors when excited by solar particles
Sunlight reflects off ice crystals at different angles near the poles
Earth's magnetic field separates white light into a spectrum of colors
